[DEFINING AI — CHUNK 1]
	Before we get into the meat of AI’s decades long history, we have to define what the endearing term “AI” actually means. According to Google, a leader in the AI bubble, “Artificial intelligence (AI) is a set of technologies that empowers computers to learn, reason, and perform a variety of advanced tasks in ways that used to require human intelligence, such as understanding language, analyzing data, and even providing helpful suggestions.” [FOOTNOTE 1 — ITEM 1] But that definition is still quite vague.
	What do the experts say then? According to Marvin Minsky, a mathematician credited for being a founder of the AI field, he “described AI as the science of creating machines or systems that use procedures which, if performed by a human, would require intelligence.” [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 1]  John McCarthy, another founder of the field of AI and the one who coined the term AI in 1955, describes it as:
“the science and engineering of making intelligent machines, especially intelligent computer programs. It is not related to the similar task of using computers to understand human intelligence, but AI does not have to confine itself to methods that are biologically observable.” [FOOTNOTE 3 — ITEM 1]
	 But that already conflicts with the definitions we have mentioned. Moreover, even experts at a symposium led by the Nobel Foundation were unable to reach a consensus on a shared definition of intelligence, let alone artificial intelligence. To add further confusion, McCarthy highlights a fallacy with the term called the “AI effect”, saying “once something begins to work reliably, [that[ we tend to stop referring to it as AI.”  For instance, we don’t call transcription apps (e.g speech to text speech) AI yet it’s almost entirely reliant on AI. Digital assistants (cortana, siri, echo, etc), the little microphone speakers built into ios and android, all use AI to transcribe words to text. Yet, we don’t commonly hear terms like “machine learning” — a subfield in AI [FOOTNOTE 4 — ITEM 1]—, "natural language processing”, or AI being used to describe it. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 2] 
	In this sense, there’s no universal definition of AI, which makes sense if you think about it. AI is an extremely broad field with applications on a variety of tasks. The methodologies and execution of AI for, say, procedurally generating terrain for a video game is entirely different compared to the execution of AI in generative content. So what is AI? It is a spectrum of layered, nuanced, and context-specific definitions that share the same core principles of committing to “moral responsibility and factual coherence.” In other words, what defines AI currently or for a specific cohort might not necessarily be labelled AI in another time or group. However, what is agreed upon, is that AI should be ethical and factually grounded (e.g. it isn’t pseudo-science bs); a sentiment that is shared by technology and sciences in other fields.  [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 3]

[SPECULATION — CHUNK 2]
	The conception of artificial intelligence dates back to the times of Aristotle (~300 BCE), where he discusses and justifies the existence of slaves that “participates in rational principle enough to apprehend, but not to have…” in a hierarchical society. Though not explicitly stated, slaves, in the definitions of Aristotle, are entities without souls essentially equating to them no more than tools capable — to some degree — of human rationale. [FOOTNOTE 5 — ITEM 1] 
Though the link between slaves, machines, and machine sentience seems contrived at first, it makes more sense to understand that slaves were considered sentient tools by definition. That’s the distinguishing difference between slaves and an inanimate object that one would consider a tool. This framework of “sentient tools”, or intelligence without autonomy, would be the precursor towards what would become machine learning.

[GENESIS — CHUNK 3]
	Fast forward to the Enlightenment (~1685-1815) is where the idea of “sentient tools” becomes more popular. Philosophers like Rene Descartes further ruminate on the possibilities of the “machine with thought”. Others, like Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, “recognized the immense potential of mechanical reasoning machines based on logical principles” and “firmly believed in their technological feasibility”. The idea would be further refined by Etienne Bonnot de Condillac in a thought experiment, where he “used the metaphor of a statue into whose head we pour particles of knowledge” to describe a theoretically sentient machine. Within the same experiment, Condillac asks an important question: “When will a state know enough to appear intelligent to an observer?”; this question will be echoed many many times again throughout the future. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 4]
	Moving to the 20th century, the concept makes its way from the scientific community to the mainstream. Author and creator of the famed Wizard of Oz, Frank Baum created a character known as Tiktok in 1907 — not to be confused with the app Tiktok. Tiktok was a mechanical man capable of doing everything a man can, except for the fact that he wasn’t living. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 5]  [SEE FIG 1] Often, Tiktok is credited to be one of the first appearances of robots in literature. [FOOTNOTE 6 — ITEM 1] The actual term “robots” comes a decade later, from a 1920 Czech play called R.U.R (Rossum’s Universal Robots) which also depicts artificial beings similar to Tiktok. The film’s name Rossum “is a deliberate play on the Czech word rozum, meaning ‘reason’ or ‘intellect’, reflecting the play’s philosophical themes and highlighting the focus on artificial rational beings.” [SEE FIG 2] [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 6]
	This era marks the first proto-definition of AI or what we would consider a definition close to what our modern perception of the word is. And sure enough, AI finally became official a few decades after.

[THE GOLDEN AGE — CHUNK 4]
	The terms Turing Machine or Turing Test might ring a few bells, especially for computer enthusiasts. They were invented by, given their namesake, Alan Turing. He was an established British mathematician, computer scientist, logician, cryptoanalyst, and philosopher whose work contributed to the foundations of modern computer science. Starting in the 1930s, Turing’s focus turned towards defined theoretical problems. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 7]The keyword here is “theoretical” as Turing would come up with essentially a thought experiment, establishing the foundation for AI. In his famous 1950 paper Computing Machinery and Intelligence, Turing asks whether discrete state machines (digital computers, not just any machine) can “think” — or essentially imitate the traits of a human. He does so by proposing a theoretical game. There are 3 players — a man, woman, and a guard; all 3 of them are confined to a separate space and therefore cannot see one another. It is the guard’s job to ask questions and figure out who is the man and who is the woman. However, one person is tasked with giving false information and the other with truthful information. Now, say you replace one person with a machine, could the guard tell if that was a machine or not? This would be known as the Turing Test, a benchmark for determining whether a machine can match up in intelligence to that of a human. The premise of the paper was to call into question — and directly counter— perspectives that machines are not capable of achieving human consciousness or sentience. Conclusively, Turing reveals that the answer to the question to be a lot more complicated and not a simple yes or no. However, besides the relative inconclusiveness, one extremely important thing to take from the discourse is that humanity can be imitated, or at least an illusion of it. Though not entirely applicable back then, this implication plus the Turing Test would have greater significance for the trajectory of AI, especially during the modern age of LLMs. I would also like to note that these ideas would not have bore fruit were it not for the late invention of digital computers (~1939-42), which expanded the capabilities and possibilities of machines drastically. [FOOTNOTE 7 — ITEM 1] 
	The term AI became canon about 6 years after Turing’s paper, at an event known as the Dartmouth Workshop/Dartmouth Summer Research Project On Artificial Intelligence (DSRPAI for short). Here we hear of John McCarthy again who, at the time, established a group of researchers of different disciplines to make and develop ideas towards creating human-level intelligent machines, or artificial intelligence as he calls it. However, there was no truly formal definition of AI beyond Turing’s interpretation. Known as Good Old Fashioned AI (GOFAI) — coined in 1985 — this now dated interpretation was highly mechanistic by nature; it equated intelligence to mostly logical operations like symbol processing (e.g. if X-then Y statements). [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 8] That’s not to say that was the only principle behind AI; DSRPAI and Turing both accounted for intelligence in more creative faculties as well. [FOOTNOTE 7 — ITEM 2] [FOOTNOTE 8 — ITEM 1].
	During this golden age of AI, several developments were made in the field. For one, the field increased its interdisciplinary roots as it developed, drawing from fields such as cybernetics, biology, philosophy, mathematics, psychology, and many other fields. 
	Secondly, the first definitive ideas around how to forward and develop towards a machine of human intelligence were set. The overarching belief, albeit abstract, was that the human brain was really just a really powerful computer; defining and discovering logical and functional relationships, increasing computing power, and providing enough data could be the means to creating an artificial human brain. This paradigm, also known as “Strong AI”, actually helped a lot in solving a lot of problems that were limited to highly intelligent individuals. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 9]
	Thirdly, AI developments switch from cold, hard logic-based approaches to more flexible and human-centric modes. According to the Oxford dictionary, the definition of heuristic is as follows: “(adj.) Of, relating to, or enabling discovery or problem-solving esp. through relatively unstructured methods such as experimentation, evaluation, trial and error, etc”. You might have heard about it through psychology, which is much the same definition, just talked through a human-oriented context. [FOOTNOTE 9 — ITEM 1] Heuristics is what governed early as well as modern AI and machine learning. By working through this trial and error method governed by some rules of thumb (e.g. rules not 100% accurate, just good enough), researchers were able to address “deeper problems of knowledge representation and helped to make it possible to move beyond the rigid formalism of earlier logic-based systems”. I want to note that this methodology emulates how heuristics work in a psychology or humanist standpoint. For instance, we know not to touch fire because we understand it burns and it hurts us. But for us to understand that, we need to have experienced it one way or the other - indirectly or directly (e.g. witnessing someone else get burnt or we get too close to a flame); the fear of fire is not innate. In that sense, learn through trial and error and develop a rule — albeit not 100% accurate as with any other heuristic — that makes us more cautious around flames or things that resemble flames. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 10]
	Fourthly, similar to the previous point, researchers realized that the logic, symbolic-based frameworks that AI ran had certain limits, effectively rendering possibilities like machine translation or natural language processing difficult to produce. In light of this, they pursued a framework that sought to “represent and manipulate explicit knowledge” by the late 60s-70s. Instead of building AI that can only interpret information in a fixed manner, researchers sought to build AI that had — and what McCarthy reasoned was essential for realizing AI — common sense. The goal then was to make a machine that could infer, deduce, induce, and reason about new connections without being explicitly told to do so. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 11] 
	Fifthly, the formal theory of AI is developed. Defined by the works of the aforementioned Minsky, the field is “the study of how the beliefs, intentions, and judgments of an intelligent agent can be appropriately expressed within a logic suitable for ‘automated reasoning’.” Or, in other words, the study of how we can emulate human intelligence (and beyond) by programming it. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 12]
	Sixthly, it becomes apparent that psychological principles guided (and would continue to influence) the path of AI development, such as with the EPAM program in 1959 — which investigated associative memory and forgetting in a computer program that emulated behaviorisms of certain psychological patients — or ELIZA in 1966, the world’s first chatbot [SEE FIG 3].  [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 13]
	Lastly, this first wave of AI gave birth to “expert systems” — “AI applications characterized by a robust, rule-based approach that relied on domain-specific knowledge in narrowly-defined areas of expertise”. These AI tools were essentially highly specialized applications meant for completing monotonous tasks that required specialized knowledge and/or skills. These tools were practical and useful, but only to a certain degree due to the aforementioned limitations of the current formalized logic-based framework, which simply couldn’t account for every possible scenario due to computational limitations. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 14]

[DEPRESSION AND THE SECOND COMING — CHUNK 5]
	Like all good times, the golden age of AI had to come to an end. Though I won’t go deep into the details, the main catalyst for what would be known as the “AI Winter” was heavy skepticism about the usefulness and practicalities of AI research. In the late 1960s , groups like the American Automatic Language Processing Advisory Committee (ALPAC) and James Lighthill called into question whether the high investments in the field would actually yield anything beneficial. Overhyped, and filled with unrealistic expectations and exaggerated ambitions, the AI field couldn’t keep up with the criticism and its own shortcomings; especially when still the majority of its methodologies remained in the logic-based GOFAI framework. Resultantly, funding was pulled from the US Congress and the British Government, leading to AI disappearing from the mainstream. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 15]
	Though the term “AI Winter” or depression, makes it sound like AI has rescinded into a stage akin to the Medieval Ages, it was far from it. Though AI had disappeared from the mainstream, it didn’t mean that it had disappeared completely. In fact, having this “reality check” shifted the overtly optimistic field into a more pragmatic domain we would consider today. In this sense, this “winter” is actually just a retrospective and reevaluative phase. Allowing for the field to focus and set up for its next breakthrough in the 90s or, in other words, the second AI boom. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 16]
	The main paradigm of this second coming centered around 4 foundational approaches to AI systems: systems that can think like humans, think rationally, act like humans, and act rationally.
	Systems that can think like humans is an improved spin on Turing’s conception of a thinking machine. In this case, researchers envisioned a machine that had an actual mind: one that could do human-like mental tasks like thinking, decision-making, learning, etc. It’s a much more generalist approach rather than focusing on a specific feature of humans. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 17]
	Systems that think rationally — according to MIT comp science professor Patrick Henry Winston — centers around ”discover[ing] how programs can learn the way people usually do — by reasoning about experiences in the light of commonsense knowledge”  [FOOTNOTE 10 — ITEM 1]. In this sense, much like the first approach, the goal is for machines to become more human-like, but through their reasoning or common sense.
	Systems that act like humans echoes Turing’s theoretical imitation game, or the “Turing Test”. This approach focused on “the art of creating machines that perform functions that require intelligence when performed by people” [FOOTNOTE 11 — ITEM 1] and “the study of how to make computers do things at which, at the moment, people are better at.” [FOOTNOTE 12 — ITEM 1] Admittedly, this approach is a bit of a misnomer as it's not as much as having systems that can imitate or replicate humans, rather it’s developing systems that can replace people in all tests of human intelligence — which is a very loose definition here.
	Systems that act rationally is the most contemporary approach. It focuses on “... the study of agents that receive percepts [e.g. perceiving something] from the environment and perform actions. Each such agent implements a function that maps percept sequences to actions … such as reactive agents, real-time planners, and decision-theoretic systems.” [FOOTNOTE 13 — ITEM 1] This approach is fundamentally the same as systems that can think like the humans, but it builds upon it, suggesting that there are a variety of machines that can achieve such rationality than just a very general conception of machines as whole.
	All 4 of these approaches would set the new framework that would finally end the long standing logic-based GOFAI approach. This common sense-based and rational framework would be exhibited through the emergence of data-driven models, particularly “artificial neural networks and large-scale machine learning architecture.” If any of that phrase sounds familiar, it’s because that’s the same technology that is powering our systems today: image recognition, language processing, autonomous systems, and other technologies are all powered by this fundamental advancement of AI. This is modern AI. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 18]

[GLOBALIZATION, BIG DATA, AND THE MODERN AGE — CHUNK 6]
	The third AI boom comes with the rise of extremely complicated and powerful technologies, the implementation of global data infrastructures (e.g. the world wide web), and — subsequently — the availability of large real-world data sets. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 19]
	Despite the large boom in innovation, AI researchers sought to steer AI in a more conservative route, choosing to refine and establish more credibility around the field. Furthermore, they redefined and set specific goals and domains for the field; establishing AI in a more grounded and practical manner than it had a few decades ago. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 20]
	Following this practicality, several types of AI architecture were pursued. For one, there was the Hidden Markov Model (HMM), a statistical mathematical model used to probabilistically determine certain behaviors given a data set(s), and which would form the backbone of innovations like text prediction, image analysis, voice recognition, etc. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 21]
	Second, there was the powerful artificial neural network model, which was more commonly known by the moniker “Deep Learning”. Deep learning models are what would form the basis for LLM’s today like ChatGPT or Gemini. The concept of artificial neural networks is tied to the concept of “Hebbian Learning”, which implies that “the connection between two neurons is strengthened when they are activated simultaneously,” and the greater concept of neuron architecture as a whole. Originating in the 1940s, the model was researched for a while until they reached a roadblock that most AI models encountered at the time: a lack of processing power and memory. However, with the technological advancements at the turn of the century, as well as the promise of large data sets, the capabilities of single layered networks expanded drastically. [FOOTNOTE 2 — ITEM 22]
	One of the most important inventions that exponentially expanded processing power came in the form of a technology originally built for a separate domain: gaming. Graphics processing units, or GPUs, are the primary component second to the central processing unit, or CPU. There’s a lot of computer lingo revolving around these parts but the only important part to understand in our case is that both items are extremely important for a computer to function properly (primarily the CPU though, it functions as the “brain” of the computer, allocating tasks to keep the computer running). The main difference between a CPU [SEE FIG 4] and a GPU [SEE FIG 5] lies in the way they execute processes, or a task. A CPU can only execute one task after another (i.e. sequentially) while a GPU can run multiple tasks in parallel, also known as parallel computing. Though parallel computation existed about the same time as the “Golden Age of AI”, to compare GPUs as equivalent to parallel computers would be wrong; GPUs were specifically built for the premise of graphics generation, hence the name GPU. The first commercial GPU — the Geforce 256 — and the actual term GPU, was released by Nvidia in 1999. What also made the GPU special besides its capabilities to run multiple processes in parallel was its programmable components. This, along with the demands of the gamers, gave rise to the development of GPU compatible shader languages like “Cg” or “Renderman’s shader language”: programming languages that were built to render graphics in real time. The integration of these flexible programming languages allowed users to implement algorithms in scientific computing (e.g. linear solvers, matrix-matrix operations, etc); which allowed for more general purpose functionalities within the GPU and inspired the General-Purpose GPU (GPGPU) computing movement. This move towards integrating GPUs for processes other than graphics is what expanded the margin of possibility for AI models like deep learning. [FOOTNOTE 14 — ITEM 1] 
	GPU usage outside of gaming finally became formalized in 2006, with the release of Nvidia’s CUDA. For those who don’t know what CUDA is, it is a program that 
“provides a development environment for creating high-performance, GPU-accelerated applications. With it, you can develop, optimize, and deploy your applications on GPU-accelerated embedded systems, desktop workstations, enterprise data centers, cloud-based platforms, and supercomputers.” [FOOTNOTE 15 — ITEM 1]
The conversation of GPUs will become important for us later when we discuss the design of contemporary and some other past AI architecture that we had missed.

[BEAUTIFUL ARCHITECTURES — CHUNK 7]
	Previously, we covered artificial neural networks, or the deep learning model of AI. They are the first truly modern form of AI, aligning more so with the 4 modern approaches of the second AI boom than the GOFAI approach. In its earliest state, these artificial neural networks were known as “perceptrons”. Though there are many iterations of the perceptrons, the most well known algorithm during its earliest developments was the “Novikoff Theorem” (1962), “which gives the conditions for the convergence of a perceptron learning algorithm in a finite number of steps”; essentially it is mathematical theorem that dictates that an input could be classified — hence the “convergence” part — within a finite number of steps (provided the training data is linearly separable, i.e. you can put them into separate groups/classes). The simplest representation of this structure goes as follows: suppose our input is an image (let’s call it X*). Initially, before we present the current input, we show an array of disparate images to train the model Xk where k = {0, 1, 2, 3…n}, with k representing the index of the current image, which is used to classify whether this value goes to class A or B. Say there is a separable divider, when k reaches the nth index. Before the nth index (e.g. 0-(n-1)), all images are classified as class A and all images after the nth index (e.g. n-some number (this training set is finite)) are classified as class B. Since it is a mathematical interpretation we need to group it in an interpretable way. So we now have a function y(x), whose argument (the x value) can accept an image of Xk and can only return a value between {-1, 1}. This function acts as the “master”, ordering the images to class A or B and returns either a value of 1 if it fits in class A or -1 if it fits in class B. A value of 0 would equate to a divider (“the hyperplane”). Since there is a clearly separable distinction in the array of images Xk, Novikoff’s theorem (and related theorems) presumes that the data is linearly separable. Now, the thing is, when we put an input to an image, ideally we won’t expect the image to truly “fit” into class A or B. Or, in other words, if we fed X* into y(x), it shouldn’t return exactly -1 or 1. Since the data is separable, we can introduce gradients with another function: the weight function W(x). By the way this is when we sort of branch off Novikoff’s initial theorem, heading onto further developments of this theorem. Much like y(x), the argument x can be any image. However, the return value now returns a value in between, and including {-1, 1}; and we can explicitly represent the equation as W(x) = wm X + w0, where m = {1, 2, 3 …} and is the index of the weight (represented by tiny w) and w0 functions essentially as a constant. So, by feeding X* into the W(x), we should expect that we can get an approximation (set by some form of error) of where image X* lies between class A and B.  [FOOTNOTE 18 — ITEM 1]
	It should be noted that this representation is an oversimplified version of Novikoff’s theorem and doesn’t cover all the nuances. 
	For one, the images are vectorized (an object that has direction and magnitude, think velocity). Secondly, though this demonstration expressed that there’s a clear index n that separates images from separating one another, there isn’t usually a certain index that determines if the image is classified to class A or B. Rather the images would be vectorized and then judged based on its geometric properties (e.g. which direction its facing and what magnitude it is in multi-dimensional space). Thirdly, y(x) is not really a function at all: rather, it would be set explicitly by the person who creates the rules and classifications. The actual math behind the theorem provides additional insight. [SEE FIG 7] 
	Further theorems and techniques would improve upon the theorem, correcting issues with each iteration. For instance, recurrent algorithms — like the “Support Vector Machine (SVM)” method or “Bregman divergence” (which is widely used today for machine learning, image segmentation, many other things) — were created, allowing for dynamic weight calibration. In short, instead of requiring a previous dataset (like the array of images in our previous example), the model will change the way it measures its weights based on every successive input. In the most abstract sense, we can see the first formulation of a proto-brain with this relatively simple, deterministic mathematical model. It is a beautiful demonstration of smart design combined with well ordered abstractions. [FOOTNOTE 18 — ITEM 2] 
	Besides this model, later iterations of perceptrons also embraced another fundamental type of model: Stochastic Approximations and Gradients. Though the equation looks simpler than Novikoff’s model [SEE FIG 8], the implications and process is about, if not just as complex. Instead of working on purely mathematical principles, Stochastic models relied on statistical methods, which moved the degree of uncertainty to an unbounded context (i.e. instead of determining convergence through a finite number of steps, convergence is determined through some point approaching infinity). This methodology wasn’t a bad thing as it allowed for the expression of errors through gradients rather than the binary classification of Novikoff: instead of expressing if an output’s quality from -1 to 1 in often large jumps, the value can be expressed through a much finer number (due to the unbounded context). Let’s take a look back at figure 8. The equation essentially represents the following: J(w) is equivalent to the performance of the output. J(w) is represented by the integral of X inputs of the cost/loss function Q(x,w) times p(x), the probability density — or how likely an input of x would appear in real life. Q(x,w) is what is actually important, showing the penalty/cost of an input x given the current weight calibrations. In the case of our images analogy again, Q(x,w) would increase if the image was classified poorly by the weights that were already set in the model. Taken in full, J(w) is equivalent to an average value of the penalty of our image times the likelihood of our image in the real world. This means the bigger J(w) is, the poorer our neural network was able to classify our judgments. This allows us to map this value to an algorithm like [SEE FIG 9]. Semantically figure 9 could be translated to: the new weight for this input w[n] equals the weights of our previous iterations minus the step size ɣ[n] (the learning rate of our model) times the highest change in penalty for this input with the current weights ∇Q(x[n], w[n-1]). Remember how I had mentioned about the granularity, or fineness, of Stochastic outputs? Instead of forcing us to adjust the weights significantly, the model can simply adjust the weight some degree towards the right direction with a higher accuracy. It’s in these subtle adjustments that Stochastic algorithms distinguish, and are more adaptable than Novikoff’s method. By design, the Stochastic model’s highly adaptive nature allows it to interpret the dynamic, chaotic, and unrestrained nature of most data sets. Because of its well designed flexibility, Stochastic models would be used in almost all AI text and image-based generators. [FOOTNOTE 18 — ITEM 3] 
	There are other significant developments within deep learning like backpropagation, which provides a much more efficient mode of updating weights with respect to error than the standard method of updating all weights simultaneously, or the previously mentioned Markov chain. But for the most part, Stochastic and Novikoff models would be the basis behind most, if not all artificial intelligence logic. Though it covers only the Diffusion-based AI architecture, I recommend taking a look at [FOOTNOTE 19 — ITEM 1] to see a more comprehensive understanding of contemporary AI mathematical models and architecture in relation to these older, fundamental models.
	Besides perceptrons, there were also the illusory AI agents of the same or future periods (e.g. 1960s-1980s) like ELIZA, PARRY, or LUNAR; hard coded “AI”, all of which functioned as chatbots that imitated a certain subgroup of people — typically within a clinical psychology setting (patients, with PARRY, and a psychologist with ELIZA; LUNAR was supposed to be akin to a geologist): or the practical, and groundbreaking expert systems agents, which were also rigorously calibrated. This is an important distinction as these 2 agents seemed “lifelike” but were really facades of true intelligence, powered by clever programming and large amounts of data and edge cases. [FOOTNOTE 16 — ITEM 1]  The architecture of such systems can be thought about in the following manner: a huge branch of logic operators (if/else; either/or statements; essentially equate these to rules/algorithms) that route to different outputs based on a large set of data, which would be arduously put in. This is particularly the case with expert systems, whose architecture is structured like this [SEE FIG 6]:
The Knowledge Base: “... the facts the system has about a subject. Expert systems are usually supplied with a huge amount of information on a particular topic. This information comes from experts, databases, electronic encyclopedias.”
Rules Set: “... sets of rules, or algorithms. These rules tell the system how to evaluate and work with the information and how to answer or approach queries from users.”
Inference Engine: This is the machine that connects the knowledge base and rules set, using both items to “interpret and evaluate the facts to provide the user with a response or solution.”
User Interface (UI): The window in which “users interact with. It allows users to enter their input and shows them the results.” Following conventional design, the UI is designed to be intuitive and easy to use.  [SEE FIG 7] [FOOTNOTE 17 — ITEM 1]
An interesting note is that expert systems and psychological chatbots function similarly in the same manner as LLMs today: a back and forth chat between user and AI, despite the internal architecture being entirely different. 
	On the topic of LLMs, I think it ought to be important to actually understand how LLMs function since they are a chronic presence and are becoming an increasingly integral part of our lives outside and within work: ~14% adoption rate for US firms, ~50% and 45% for individuals (non-work and work-based respectively). [FOOTNOTE 20 — ITEM 1] Moreover, LLMs have a powerful logical architecture — the “transformer” — that is based on a different mathematical model. When we input a prompt, say “Hello, how are you?” The input is then broken into smaller word subunits, called tokens, in a process known as “tokenization”. You could think of this as normalizing the input so that the machine can interpret it easier. Then they are vectorized (e.g. words turn into math) through a process called embedding. The embedding process is dynamic and so, as more data is fed into an LLM, the values will be translated slightly differently with each subsequent iteration. These vectors are then transformed into 3 other vectors denoted by Q, K, and V. Q for query, essentially what the token wants. K is for key, which represents the information each token contains. And V is value, which — when accessed — returns the key multiplied by an attention weight. At this point, we’ve already entered the transformer part of the model. [FOOTNOTE 21 — ITEM 1] 
	Transformer architecture is one of the most revolutionary discoveries within modern AI, opening a gateway for natural language processing that could not be achieved before. The mathematical model underlying transformers utilizes “Scaled Dot-Product Attention”, which looks like the following [SEE FIG 10]. The equation basically reads as the following: the output (Attention) of Q, K, V — the vectors we had mentioned previously — equals the softmax (a function representing a probability of distribution, e.g. range of values) of QKT, the dot product of Q times the transpose of K (this is known as attention), times the scaling factor 1dk (to account for reduced gradients) times V. In layman’s terms, the dot product of Q and transpose K helps us map each token to one another; so like matching words of a sentence together and scoring how well they fit together. We then multiply it by the scaling factor to make sure that there is still room for error/uncertainty. This is a tad bit different from the analogy of stochastic gradients we introduced, where gradients are an intrinsic part of the method; in this case, a gradient exists but not for all cases (e.g. QKT is huge), thus a scaling factor is created to preserve it. Despite this distinction, stochastic models are still deeply related with Scaled Dot-Production Attention and transformers in general, providing the engine for its calculations. And then we map those products to the value pairs to relate the matches, since V returns what K represents. To further increase accuracy, this operation is run through the “Multi-Head Attention” approach. Basically, this operation is then operated h amount of times iteratively (with each successive iterations having slightly different values) in the dimensions of Q, K, V, resulting in a V dimensional output values. These values are then concatenated (added together one after the other) and run through the function again to get the final output. Admittedly, the Scaled Dot-Product Attention model is not as clean as compared to stochastic approximations, but that is to be expected given the complexity of the task it's trying to achieve — which is achieving (arguably) true semantic understanding. Regardless, it’s still a very powerful architecture that would be used in many revolutionary types of AI, more on that later. [FOOTNOTE 22 — ITEM 1]

[THE UGLY — CHUNK 8]
	Despite the potential upsides of modern AI systems and architecture, AI tends to carry a rather controversial reputation towards the masses. For some of us, it’s quite a useful everyday tool. For others, it’s a bad omen (job laborers beware). We know that AI in the workforce is a bit of a finicky topic, especially in light of the increasing layoffs due to AI. As of 2026, “AI was the leading reason U.S.-based employers cited for layoffs in March” accounting for “25% of layoffs tracked in March and 13% in the first quarter, up from 5% throughout”. As of recently, Snap — the company who created Snapchat — made headlines for mass layoffs (~16% of its current workforce) due to AI. [FOOTNOTE 23 — ITEM 1] Or the amusing memes about AI, water usage, and sustainability. [SEE FIG 11] Or even the entire outrage of AI artworks and the ensuing protest against it a while back (e.g. the no AI campaign on popular art platforms like Artstation, DeviantArt, etc). [SEE FIG 12] But those issues are old news, the ugly side of AI has grown a lot since then. 
	The rabbit hole is a bit deep so we’ll just tackle some prominent ones that maybe you might’ve encountered before. The term “AI slop” is used to refer to “low-quality, mass produced content that is surreal, uncanny, or simply grotesque” and that may contain “a brief, rudimentary plot”. Think of steroid infused anthropomorphic cats, anthropomorphic fruits causing a lover’s affair, etc. Anything that was weird, uncanny, and confusing was probably covered by the mass of AI slop at some point. [SEE FIG 13] Over the past year, AI slop has proliferated all mainstream social media, through apps like Instagram, Tiktok, YouTube, etc. It became so common that it prompted YouTube to take action, demonetizing and removing accounts who generated what was essentially addictive, but ultimately garbage content (hence the large viewer count). Experts called this mass proliferation of AI junk the next wave of “enshittification”, a term used to describe the declining experience of online users as platforms become increasingly concerned about high quality content. [FOOTNOTE 24 — ITEM 1]
	Following in the same direction of AI slop and layoffs, large companies have turned towards using gen AI to make their ads. Large profile companies, like Coca Cola, have been repeatedly attempting to incorporate entirely AI generated content for their advertisements to cut costs and labor. This has not been met with open arms though, as its holiday ad back in 2024 received heavy backlash despite the large engagement [SEE FIG 14]. Personally, it’s fairly obvious the content is AI given the surrealistic cuts, camera panning, and the generated models; which removes the charm of the ads. [FOOTNOTE 25 — ITEM 1] Svedka, the vodka company, also recently decided to do a similar thing with their long, retired feminine robot mascot; creating an AI ad that aired in Superbowl LX [SEE FIG 15]. Like Coca Cola, reactions were mixed and confused. [FOOTNOTE 26 — ITEM 1] This is not to say pursuing AI usage in advertising was bad: in fact, in 2023, coke made an ad that infused AI elements with real footage called “The Masterpiece”, which wasn’t met with the heavy criticisms that it suffered from its Christmas ad. However, going 100% AI is still something that doesn’t seem any better than the funky AI slop permeating out feeds. [FOOTNOTE 25 — ITEM 2]
	Besides the generative AI content, existing machine learning algorithms have gotten significantly better. If you remembered about Peele’s deep fake video of Obama back in 2018 [SEE FIG 16], it was a pretty convincing representation of Obama until, of course, Peele revealed himself. The general premise revolved around misinformation and how the implications of AI technologies like deep fakes should be considered seriously. Well, since then, deep fakes have gotten tremendously better. Deep fakes are essentially what they sound like, deep learning models (like general adversarial networks (GANs), the precursor to diffusion based image generation, combined with autoencoders) that allow users to manipulate facial expressions, replace faces, create new faces, and even mimic speech. [FOOTNOTE 27 — ITEM 1] Deep fakes within the Peele era still had caveats: they tend to err towards poor resolution to avoid defects from being detected early as seen in fig 16. However, no longer is artificial noise needed; deep fake videos are now almost indistinguishable from the real thing. AI platforms like Viewmax.io allow anyone to superimpose someone else’s body and face onto them. A popular content creator using said deep fake technologies is kuba.creates.AI on tiktok or instagram, who clones famous people. [SEE FIG 17] The implications of such powerful technologies that are readily accessible are tremendous and should be further explored, especially when the fake is indistinguishable from real.

[THE PRETTY — CHUNK 9]
	Despite the problems that come with AI, there’s always the upsides as well. Because of its interdisciplinary background, AI has — to a high degree of success — advanced many scientific, creative, and social fields; improving ease of life and providing key information with powerful implications. Like the ugly side, there’s a lot to go explore here. As such, we’ll cover some prominent ones pushing the envelope of what could be achieved with the help of AI tools.
	AlphaFold was one of the greatest applications of AI within the domain of science, particularly biology and all related fields. AlphaFold was essentially a 3D image modelling software that could fold proteins in 3D space accurately using deep learning models. [SEE FIG 18] The reason why folding proteins in 3D space beyond being graphed in standard amino chains in 2D space was due to the fact that the way it “folded” in 3D space determined many of its core functionalities. To name a few, the 3D structure of proteins determines how they bind, channel, and receive other molecules or how they interact with another to form much more complex structures built for structural and regulatory purposes. And these structures are extremely precise, leaving room for a small margin of error. The issue before AlphaFold was that being able to accurately draw a 3D protein structure from its 1D or 2D structure was extremely difficult since the possible ways proteins folded were large. According to Levinthal’s paradox: “Finding the native folded state of a protein by a random search among all possible configurations can take an enormously long time.Yet proteins can fold in seconds or less.” There were methods that could detail a protein in high accuracy — like X-ray crystallography, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy, electron microscopy, etc — but they were expensive and timely. Computer-based 3D structure prediction was the next route, but most methods were still highly inaccurate in their predictions. [FOOTNOTE 28 — ITEM 1] 
This 50 year old problem to accurately predict protein 3D structures was finally solved with AlphaFold 2 in the 2020 CASP-14 (Critical Assessment of Protein Structure Prediction) — which was essentially a competition for protein structure prediction technologies to accurately predict certain protein structures. Since then, AlphaFold has predicted over 200 million protein structures, something that could’ve taken “hundreds of years to solve experimentally”. The implications of this are massive as accurate protein predictions can lead to better understanding of diseases and drug discovery. According to Google, AlphaFold provided their “first major proof point that AI can be a powerful tool to advance science.” [FOOTNOTE 29 — ITEM 1]
	In the creative domain, AI has been incorporated into various tools, providing streamlined workflows and reducing production time significantly. Besides the video generative tools, there’s also lesser known tools with powerful impact in the industry. 
	For one, there’s Cascadeur, a powerful 3D animation tool that uses AI to enhance physics simulations, provide keyframe interpolation (e.g. provide the “in-between” frames to make animations smoother), convert mocap footage into animations, and do much more.  [SEE FIG 19] This tool provides significant decreases in time investment for traditional animations, allowing for more to be completed in a short span of time. [FOOTNOTE 30 — ITEM 1]
	Another powerful invention, although currently memed and met with skepticism for its rather uncanny look, is DLSS 5 by NVIDIA. DLSS 5 is the most recent iteration of DLS, which are deep learning empowered technologies designed to optimize game performance while maintaining graphical fidelity. Separating from the old iterations of DLSS, which were designed to upscale resolutions and interpolate new frames, DLSS 5 provides consistent, photorealistic inter-frame superimposition and rendering. In other words, DLSS 5 makes games look super realistic by calculating things like lighting and the way materials are supposed to render in real time. Furthermore, DLSS 5 is modular, allowing game devs and artists alike to change the way the AI model renders their game; allowing full agency in terms of artistic constraints. It’s essentially a significantly better, optimized, and real-time version of video generation tools. [SEE FIG 20] Though it’s still a bit surreal in the imagery, DLSS 5 and successive iterations hold a lot of promise in the gaming industry. [FOOTNOTE 31 — ITEM 1] 
	Continuing with NVIDIA, generative physical AI (AI that can navigate a virtual environment) is getting a revamp with fVDB. [SEE FIG 18] fVDB is an open-source framework that allows AI to learn large amounts of sparse 3D data at an extremely rapid pace. Compared to previous similar frameworks or libraries of similar function, fVDB provides a unified API (e.g. you don’t have to install numerous applications to run it) and a more efficient program overall (4x the spatial scale, 3.5x faster, and has 10x more operators). The implications are big within 3D mapping and visualization (like photogrammetry), allowing generative physical AI models to achieve better performance when it comes to real-time mapping, say creating a visual representation (with accurate physical simulations) of an area that is too toxic for humans to access and only machines can achieve. [FOOTNOTE 32]
